desc:
A tall hill, with a bare rocky crest extends above the surrounding terrain. On
the top of the hill, low-growing shrubs have taken root in the rocky ground. The
hill is mostly bush covered, with some scattered pines and larches. From the
top, the view shows the tower hills spreading out for miles in all directions
except towards the southeast, where the hills give way to fields.
name: Crest of a Hill

desc:
The trail bends here to pass along the side of a rocky escarpment. Huddled
together in the leeward side of the rocks a few stunted pine trees struggle for
an existence in the rocky soil. The trees stand silent and unmoving as
travellers pass them by. A small footpath can be seen leading off to the east
where a solitary wooden cabin has been built.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Sombre tones of green and purple colour the featureless landscape of the hills.
The rough grasslands form mosaics on the thin, sandy soils of the higher,
steeper slopes and broken ground. On the lower slopes, a few stunted birches
grow amidst shrubs and undergrowth. A small number of smooth sandstone rocks are
scattered about the hills.
name: Rugged Hills

desc:
In a small dell between surrounding grassy hills; rusted and broken tools from
past mining activities clutter the area, half-buried in the dirt. Large blocks
of grey granite, the remains of a ruined building, lie here in the grass. To the
south, a steep rock face, covered in dull-coloured grass and moss, looms above
the dell.
name: Above an Old Mine

desc:
A craggy hill of grey rock, blanketed with reddish moss projects well above the
background of rolling hills. From the bare hilltop, there is an unbroken view of
the surrounding area. The foot of the hill is covered with low-growing and bushy
pine trees and a dense growth of prickly bushes. A stack of stones has been
erected at summit of the hill.
name: Hilltop

desc:
Ranges of flat-topped, rugged hills dominate the landscape here. The craggy
hillsides are partly covered with vegetation, mostly patches of short grass and
red-coloured shrubs. The lower hills are covered with heath, and a few pine
trees grow in the stony soil at the foot of the hills.
name: Rugged Hills

desc:
Moss covered rocks crown this windswept hillside. To the lea of the hill, small
brackish pools have formed in the hollows; clearly drainage is a problem and
only the hardier varieties of grass and shrub have found a home here. To the
south a small thicket of pine trees breaks the monotony of the stony ground.
name: Windswept Hillside

desc:
The ground is bearably steep, rising from the valley to the north to meet the
ridgeline that extends as far as the eye can see to the south. A few heather
plants push themselves up from between the rocks spreading, unchallenged, across
the ground.
name: A Steep Ridgeline

desc:
A thin, brown thread marks the passage of the trail across the green, undulating
terrain. The wide, rutted wagon-trail snakes its way between the hills and
valleys, picking its path along the contours of the land. At the side of the
trail a broken, mouldy wagon-wheel rots away, providing a home for ants and
other insects.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The fertile hills support a variety of flora, most of it varieties of grasses
that fight for supremacy among the stunted bushes. The lack of animal grazing
here has allowed the grass to grow tall; high enough to cause even the tallest
of men some difficulty. To the south, a busy track cuts across the land.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The ground underfoot is covered with an abundance of bushy scrub. None of the
plants are particularly tall but the thick, woody stems are a trip hazard to the
unwary. The rolling hills to the west and south are mottled with browns and
greens and stretch as far as the eye can see.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The various types of scrub competing for areas to grow in have created an
unusual pattern in the countryside. Mottled squares of green and brown appear to
have been draped across the landscape like a giant patchwork quilt. Several
woody stems encircle a lone tree on the western slopes. To the north, the cliff
has a narrow opening at the base, save for that, the area is unremarkable.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The ground underfoot is somewhat wet and large areas of moss cling to the
rolling grass. Between two of the small hills a valley is crossed with dark,
black trenches where peat has been cut. To the side of a trench a peat stack has
been left to dry and a pungent, smoky aroma emanates from it.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The rise and fall of the surrounding grassland would tire even the strongest of
wagon horses. Perhaps that is why the trail passes through a deep cutting in the
hills allowing the track to stay almost level. Some of the earth forming the
banks of the cutting has crumbled and collected at the base of the bank as the
wind and rain have eroded it away over many months, the detritus forming a long,
thin pile of muck at the side of the trail.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Rising sharply to dominate the low ground that surrounds it, this lofty peak
affords an excellent view of the valley that curves around its base and the
sweeping trail that traverses the flat lands below. A small thicket of pine
trees grows at an angle as if leaning into the prevailing wind. Little else,
save for the occasional shrub, grows in the rocky soil.
name: A Rock Strewn Peak

desc:
A sharp turn in the trail to pass between two giant boulders has forced the
track to narrow, barely wide enough for a fully laden cart to pass. Forcing all
traffic to pass along the exact same path has badly worn the surface of the
track and someone has tried to repair the potholes by filling them with sand and
crushed rocks.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Hard-packed earth gives way to loose, dusty soil covering a series of slab-like
rocks that have been used in the construction of the trail. The surface is
treacherous to walk on and tired travellers need to take extra care not to twist
an ankle. Thickets of gorse have taken hold at the side of the path making it
difficult to leave the main trail save for a narrow path that has been hacked
through the vegetation to the south.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
A stream cuts across the trail leaving pools of muddy, brown water to fill the
ruts in the trail so that it is no longer possible to see how deep some of these
grooves are. Further up the hill to the west the source of the stream can be
seen, twisting and turning as it flows down the slope. Circles of blackened ash
ringed by stones lie along the trail indicating that someone used this spot as a
campsite recently.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Verdant, green grass is spread across the hill with a dense growth of ruddy
shrubs sheltering beneath a rocky outcrop on the western side. The ground slopes
steeply down to a hollow to the south. On the eastern side it looks as if the
hill itself has been worn to the bone, the pale greys and whites of the exposed
rock is in stark contrast to the rich colours on display here.
name: Rugged Hills

desc:
This exposed plateau stops abruptly at a vertical drop that forms the northern
and eastern edge; even an experienced climber would struggle to make a descent
as the desolate summit lacks anything to which a rope might be secured. The edge
of the precipice is carpeted with russet moss which contrasts sharply with the
dull grey surroundings.
name: Top of a Crag

desc:
The entrance to a tunnel lies to the north, rough-cut in the rock face. The
remains of a broken mining cart sit near the tunnel entrance. By the look of
things, this old mine has been abandoned for a long time. A few large, jagged
chunks of rock lie beneath the moss-covered rock face.
name: Before an Entrance to a Tunnel

desc:
The cabin is simple yet functional. There are few home comforts here, the owner
seems to value practicality above all else. A fireplace on the eastern wall is
surrounded with piles of seasoned wood, a stockpile that should last the winter.
Metal hooks attached to the wooden rafters are used to hang kills although they
are all empty.
name: Hunter's Cabin

desc:
The trail curves gently across the valley between two hilly areas, carving out a
wide, russet arch on the face of the landscape. Half-submerged rocks and
potholes mark the surface of the trail and the deep grooves formed by the
passing wagon wheels turn and twist to miss the obstacles. Flowering shrubs and
small rocks dot the surroundings and the remains of a large animal has been
picked clean by scavenging birds and left at the edge of the trail.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The rolling hills stretch into the distance and the view is interrupted only by
a few thickets of bushy oak trees and thorny shrubs. The various clefts and
hollows formed by the ground provide ample cover for the small mammals that make
this area home.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The undulating peaks are carpeted with long, tufted grass and groves of untamed
shrubs. The hills to the north are crowned with a makeshift wire fence that has
been repaired many times and is in dire need of replacement. The lack of trees
or cover of any other kind makes this particular area feel very exposed.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The uniform greenery covering most of the gently rolling hills is broken up with
large tracts of scorched and blackened vegetation. The burnt scrub is the result
of a wild fire that swept through this area some time ago. Already, tiny white
flowers have begun to flourish amid the charred remains showing that nature
cannot be held back for long.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
Stunted bushes and feeble trees are scattered across the rolling hills with only
a thin carpet of grass holding the dark soil together. The land appears to be
quite inhospitable except for small mammals that dart back and forth among the
undergrowth.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
A prominent ridge of hills that encircles this region forms a natural bowl that
draws the eye towards a rocky prominence that erupts from the centre of the
hollow. The boulders are remarkable as the vast majority of the hills here are
formed from fertile peat that supports the diverse range of scrub that covers
the ground.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The ground here slopes gently to the east into a deep valley. Despite the slope
the drainage is poor, probably as a result of the peat in the soil which makes
the ground soft underfoot and tiring to walk on. Clumps of matted grass and
thorny shrubs are all that grow here.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The flora here offers no surprises, it is a continuation of the same vegetation
that stretches for miles around. Tufts of long grasses are interspersed with
small, wiry shrubs while a few varieties of flowers have managed to find a place
to grow.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
A seam of peat has been dug from the soft ground leaving a dark, black scar
through the vegetation that stretches across the rolling hills. Several neat
peat stacks have been left to dry in the sun and faint wagon wheel marks can be
seen leading up to them.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
A few trees cling to the exposed hillside, their trunks bent and twisted as they
try to grow against the prevailing winds which sweep across the hills. The scrub
hills continue far into the north while to the south a narrow trail winds its
way across the landscape.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The trail stretches straight and true across the countryside, as far as the eye
can see a thin brown line marks the route as it rises and falls across various
ridges and valleys on the landscape. Some thin, woody scrub grows in the dirt
along the centre of the trail but the hard-packed ruts, compacted under hundreds
of passing wagon wheels are barren of any vegetation.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Two deep ruts run in parallel through these hills. The gap is scarcely wider
than a man's outstretched arms indicating that only smaller wagons have
travelled this route. The grasses and scrub bushes that flourish here have been
worn away along this trail, both within the ruts and between them; the exposed
dirt is hard-packed and smooth. The trail curves slightly as it climbs a low
hill, avoiding a sparse tree-line; otherwise, it follows a straight path from
east to west, rising and falling with the terrain.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
Two trails converge at this crossroads, to the north the farming village of
Black Hill can be seen atop the crest of the hill while to the east a wagon
trail, well-used by merchants, leads off across the hills. The wide path heading
south wends its way through Emyn Beraid, a route favoured by those heading to
the Grey Havens.
name: South of Black Hill Village

desc:
An old path weathered and aged leads to a major junction to the north. Heavy
traffic has taken its toll on the surface of the path leaving ruts and potholes
in the centre of the road. The path winds its way to the west leaving a black
scar on the landscape. The Tower Hills lie to the east and south, lush with
greenery, game and all manner of life.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The road shifts direction as the current route has run into a hill too steep to
climb. The rough path is marked with various wheel ruts from wagons that have
attempted to use the route while overloaded. Small animals can be seen scurrying
about. The hills provide them with good cover to hide from airborne predators
circling high above.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The road cuts through the hills to the north and south, hugging the contour
lines to make the journey as easy as possible. This wide path is broad enough to
support heavy traffic and judging by the state of the ruts in the road, heavy
traffic, passing in all weathers, is exactly what this road got. To the west the
hills rise too steeply to be climbed. To the east the fabled Tower Hills extend
as far as the eye can see.
name: Deeply-rutted Wagon Trail

desc:
The ground here rises and falls with regularity, creating low hills and shallow
valleys. Hill and valley alike are covered with low, woody bushes spaced at
irregular intervals. Most of these bushes would reach as high as the waist on a
man and seem to be thriving in this area; some, however, are little more than
piles of bare sticks, splayed outwards as they rise from the ground.
name: Scrub-covered Hills

desc:
The path here has been paved in stone to support the wagons with farm goods
heading from the village to the trade-routes to the south. As the road enters
the village to the north it narrows slightly to pass between the shops and
houses that crowd the edges of the path.
name: Path before Black Hill Village

desc:
Single-story dwellings flank each side of the path. Through the windows, plain
wooden furniture can be seen. Faded and flaking paint adorns the woodwork around
the windows and doors of the homes while baskets of flowers hang from hooks set
into the walls. Clearly the owners of these properties are very house-proud, if
a little poor.
name: Inside Black Hill Village

desc:
A small cart with a simple canvas cover has been left at the side of the path.
Behind it several barrels have been piled up ready to be loaded. The house to
the west has a small gate at the side, behind which a small, private garden can
be seen. The path widens to the east as the street opens onto the main village
square.
name: Junction in the Path

desc:
The road turns here to pass between the tall homes on either side of the road.
High above, a laundry line stretches across the street, the bed sheets pegged to
it flapping in the wind. At street level, the faded shutters of the houses creak
as they pull against their hooks holding them open.
name: Bend in the Path

desc:
Centuries ago this area might have been overgrown with trees. Petrified remains
of several former trees are still visible even with the current vegetation
attempting to grow over the ancient roots. The Tower Hills can be seen extending
their reach to the east. To the west and north an ancient path winds its way
through the valleys.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The grass has grown taller on this individual hill and care must be taken when
forcing a route through it as the rocks and predators hiding within the brush
could be disturbed at any moment. This one hill appears larger than most,
affording an undisturbed view of the surrounding landscape and the road passing
by to the north. This ridgeline continues to the southeast leading deeper into
the Tower Hills.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
These hills are almost identical to the grassy hills that can be seen stretching
away to the north and east. There is virtually no vegetation here except for
short grass and no unique landmark to differentiate this set of hills from any
other, just more rocks and grass. The hills themselves seem old, worn smooth
over time.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The lush, rolling Tower Hills converge on the mouth of the canyon. What starts
as a gentle slope leads to a long descending path to the south. This area is
teeming with vegetation and game. The greatness of the Tower Hills is swallowed
by the shale canyon formation extending southward.
name: North end of a Canyon

desc:
The remnants of large boulders, fractured by time and weather make up several
high mounds now covered in moss. In the lee of the mounds a thicket of trees has
found enough shelter from the prevailing wind to grow strong and tall. As the
trees gained height they climbed above the shelter of the mounds and were
exposed to the strong winds that can sometimes blow across this peak. One
unfortunate tree has succumbed to the force of nature and toppled, its roots
erupting from the ground. Where the roots have torn free, clumps of dirt rest
loosely on top of the grass. A path can be seen to follow along to the north and
west while the hills continue to climb to the east and south.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
A multitude of shades of green covers the surrounding brush and tree-strewn
hills. Dense undergrowth flourishes on the gentle slopes making travelling
tiresome. A number of large rocks are jutting out from the hillside, looking
like giant teeth. Dark green moss and pale grey lichen envelop the stones.
name: Brush-covered Hills

desc:
A steep cliff to the north rises high above the surrounding landscape. Some
weathered lichen covered rocks are scattered about the area. Tall, thick grass
blankets the rugged terrain while oblong-leaved bushes with yellow flowers
thrive on the rocky ground bringing some colour to the surroundings. Wiry and
young rowan trees grow here sparsely.
name: Rocky Pasture

desc:
Shades of greys and a few streaks of rusty brown from water trickling through
the cracks, colour a high, steep rock face. A semi-circular outcrop of rock,
overhanging a cliff wall provides some shelter from the elements. The vegetation
is sparse here, only occasional patches of grass and shrubs of low height grow
on the otherwise barren, rocky ground.
name: Under a Small Ledge

desc:
The ground here is rugged and rocky, and there is little vegetation in the
barren soil. A tall rock-face overlooks the landscape, obstructing travel to the
north. Grey, angular rocks large and small litter the ground among patches of
dry, yellow grass. The terrain slopes gently down towards a boggy glen to the
south.
name: Rocky Grounds

desc:
Wooden tables have been set on the grass in this garden allowing those staying
at the inn to eat and drink outdoors when the weather permits. A simple stone
wall running around the edge of the grass keeps this area private. Faint sounds
of horses neighing and whinnying can be heard from mounts tethered in the
stables to the south.
name: The Foaming Mug's Beer Garden

desc:
This large room is filled with long, wooden tables and simple benches running
along each side. The locals appear to be making great use of this inn with most
tables taken and the room filled with sounds of chattering and clinking of mugs.
Set into the wall is a large, open fire which burns the local peat providing
plenty of heat and a distinctive, smoky aroma.
name: Common Room of The Foaming Mug

desc:
This kitchen is crowded and disorganized, but quite clean. Shelves line the
walls, stacked with pots, pans, flat dishes, and all manner of kitchen utensils.
More dishes sit on the large table that fills the centre of this kitchen; many
contain dishes in various stages of preparation. A large stone oven dominates
the northern wall, with a chimney that rises up through the ceiling; when lit,
it provides enough heat to make this small room stiflingly hot. A rickety and
steep set of wooden stairs in the northwest corner of the kitchen descends
below.
name: Tavern's Kitchen

desc:
This underground cellar is cramped and musty. Low, table-like shelves run the
length of every wall, broken only by the doorway to the staircase that ascends
back up to the kitchens. The shelves are stacked high with casks, covered
baskets, jugs, and all manner of bags and boxes; most of these clearly contain
foodstuffs or drink, but some are sealed, unmarked, and opaque. The floor
underfoot is dirt, hard-packed by the passage of feet over time.
name: Tavern's Cellar

desc:
Rows of simple wooden doors leading to the guest rooms are accessed from a long
corridor behind a small counter. A small brass bell rests on the counter to
summon the innkeeper if he is away from his post. Covering the floor is a
threadbare rug which, although worn, deadens the noise coming from the busy
common room below.
name: Inn's Private Rooms

desc:
A long corridor leads to the guest rooms of the inn. Each door has been
individually styled but only the door to the west has been adorned with a notice
hanging from a long iron nail. Large, varnished wooden columns flank the central
hallway providing support for the roof. Relief carvings have been crudely cut
into each of the columns in an attempt to provide some decoration in this
otherwise plain room.
name: Corridor to the Guestrooms

desc:
This T-shaped bedroom has space for a comfortable bed and an oak desk but is
otherwise quite cramped. In order to make it fit, the desk has been tightly
jammed into the space between the side of the bed and the door. The window is
propped open to allow fresh air into the room and the curtains flutter gently in
the breeze. Hard, polished wooden floorboards and bare walls, completely
unadorned save for a single faded painting and a set of hooks, result in noises
echoing around the room.
name: Master Bedroom

desc:
As comfortable as any other room in the inn, the bedroom provides all the
essentials but little else. While a long way from being cramped, the room is
small and appears not to have been used recently. The northern wall is mostly
occupied by a bookshelf although it would appear that it is here because it is
being stored by the inn's owners rather than as part of the room's usual
fixtures and fittings.
name: Guest Bedroom

desc:
This guest room is barely seven feet across, by the time the bed has taken its
place in the corner the room begins to look small rather than cosy. A small
patch of damp can be seen at the top of the wall, and the roof of the inn is
seemingly in need of some attention. Close to the doorway stands a cabinet where
the guests can leave their belongings.
name: Guest Bedroom

desc:
Sloping steeply down as it leads to the farmland to the north, the road passes
through a residential area where many of the inhabitants of Black Hill have
chosen to live. The homes bordering the road differ from those elsewhere in the
village. Their ground floor walls are made of stone with only the upper floors
constructed of wood.
name: Sloping Village Path

desc:
A short fence bordering a home here allows a glimpse of a small but well-tended
garden behind the property. Neatly dug flower beds border a square lawn
indicating that someone takes great pride of their garden. Across the road the
shutters are firmly closed and barred on one house, the occupant presumably away
on business.
name: Sloping Village Path

desc:
The cobbles that make up the paved surface of the road abruptly stop and give
way to a small dirt path leading north into the fields. A few homes have been
built here to give easy access to the farmland, the majority of buildings that
make up the village of Black Hill lie to the south. A sign standing next to a
door to the east indicates that a herbal shop has been set up in one of the
homes.
name: Muddy Village Path

desc:
A narrow path runs from south to north through the grasslands here, nothing more
than bare dirt where the grass has been worn away by passing feet. As the path
heads northwards it separates two rows of farmed fields with straight lines of
crops that rise tall on either side. The crops appear to be well-tended, they
are uniform in size and appear healthy. To the south a small village can be
seen, the buildings rising tall above the relatively flat ground and
gently-rolling hills that stretch away toward the horizon.
name: Path into Well-tended Fields

desc:
A small field of crops has been sown here among the short grasses of the
surrounding plains. No wall or fence surrounds the field, but a border row of
bare dirt outlines the crops and keeps the encroaching prairie grasses at bay.
Along this border, and in the dirt between the planted crops, few weeds can be
spotted; this field appears to be actively tended.
name: A Well-tended Field

desc:
Huge bellows feed air to the furnace that dominates this room, the oppressive
heat radiating from it takes some time to adjust to. A large, iron anvil sits,
unused, beside a thick, wooden water-trough. Various farm tools have been
plunged into the cooling water causing wisps of steam to rise up from the
surface. Samples of the smith's finished work, everything from horseshoes to
crosscut saws, line the walls.
name: Blacksmith's Workshop

desc:
Several small fields of crops are set here amongst the short grasses of the
surrounding plain. All have been planted in neat rows with a narrow strip of
bare dirt separating the different varieties. The more mature crops have grown
tall enough to hide the ground completely. A single, ruined section of a stone
wall runs alongside several adjoining fields. The rocks that have been dislodged
by the wind and rain have been carefully gathered and stacked against the base
of the wall to the west.
name: Well-tended Fields

desc:
Several large fields border each other here, separated by low stone walls. The
walls, stacked without mortar of any type, rise only high enough to be visible
above the rows of plants that stretch the length of the fields. Few weeds can be
spotted among the plants, or in the bare dirt between the rows; it is clear that
these fields are being actively farmed. A small wooden farmhouse can be seen a
short distance to the north, conspicuous among the fields as the only object of
significant height.
name: Well-tended Fields

desc:
Perfect rows of planted vegetables line the entire field. The neatly-packed dirt
is divided into symmetrical rectangles with narrow trails separating the crops.
There are a number of root crops growing here. In the southernmost end of the
field cereal is growing tall and dense while carrots are flourishing in the
eastern part of the field.
name: Lush Fields

desc:
A wide path leads across this yard, connecting the crop fields to the east with
the farmhouse to the west. A well-kept lawn with trimmed grass and a few trees
flanks the gravel path while a high wooden fence outlines the entire yard,
starting from both sides of the house to form a large square with only one
opening for the path to the east.
name: Front Yard

desc:
Several rows of crops run in parallel lines here, separated by rows of bare
dirt. This alternating pattern of greens and browns stands out from the
surrounding grassland, which is more uniform in colour. The crops appear to be
well-tended, the plants looking healthy and vibrant. A low stone wall runs along
all borders of this field, outside the prairie grasses grow tall and wild,
forming clumps that nearly hide the simple stone-work from view.
name: A Well-tended Field

desc:
Tall, golden cereal rises from the brown, fertile soil. The stalks grow closely
together, forming a large shimmering field that appears to stretch endlessly
towards the north, east, and west. The southern end of the field ends abruptly
at a rugged, rocky hill which rises too steeply to climb. A small trail runs
through the middle of the field, allowing access to the crop without trampling
the plants.
name: Lush Fields

desc:
Large, flat fields made up of dark-brown, loamy earth stretch in all directions.
A narrow dike which may contain water during rainy weather runs between the
different sections of the fields. Tall grass, wheat and a variety of cereal are
growing in organised patterns. A small trail crossing the fields from the east
leads on toward large barns to the west and south.
name: Lush Fields

desc:
Two large, flat fields stretch out here, separated by a low stone wall running
north-south along a narrow dirt path. The fields have been planted with straight
rows of crops alternating with rows of bare dirt; the crops appear to be
well-tended, suggesting that these fields are being actively farmed. In the
field to the east of the stone wall, a deep drainage ditch cuts across the
regular lines; it is overgrown along its edges and the bushy weeds growing tall
in the wet ground look out of place next to the carefully planted rows that
continue around them.
name: Well-tended Fields

desc:
Rows and rows of ten-by-ten feet boxes line this big, wooden building, each and
every one with their own water trough. There is room for at least thirty pigs
here but most of the boxes are empty and do not seem to have been occupied
recently. The air here is humid and thick with the odours of mud, dirt and
animal excrement.
name: In a Pigsty

desc:
This cabin is small and the ceiling is low. Built from stone, the blocks have
been packed tightly with some sort of mortar. In places the mortar has crumbled
leaving small gaps that encourage drafts. The cabin is split into two areas
separated by a thin, wooden partition. The larger of the two contains a
fireplace and table, and the smaller, nothing more than a simple bed. Both areas
have been kept fastidiously clean.
name: Low Stone Cabin

desc:
Several small fields of crops border a solitary rut through the surrounding
grasslands. The rut appears to be a simple foot-worn path through the grass, a
slightly-winding trail that has been passed over frequently enough to wear down
to bare dirt. The fields this path runs alongside are fenced in by low stone
walls, straight-running piles of rocks that barely rise above the grasses they
border. The walls must be used as boundary markings only, as they are too low to
hinder entrance to the fields.
name: Well-tended Fields

desc:
Several neglected farm fields rise from the surrounding grassland, visible as
rows of overgrown plants now gone to seed. The rows, once straight, are now
broken up by bushy clumps of tall weeds that have been allowed to run amok. The
remains of a low stone wall outlines one such field; the rocks have been spread
both into the overgrown crops and out among the bordering grasses with a
randomness suggestive of time, weather, and passing animals. An old windmill
stands to the north.
name: Poorly-tended Fields

desc:
What used to be several small farm fields dot this section of the prairie. They
have been long neglected and are now gone to seed; they are identifiable only by
the relatively straight rows the overgrown crops still form. In places even
these rows are interrupted, sometimes by mounds of dirt left behind by burrowing
animals, and sometimes by larger shrubs, fast-growing weeds that have flourished
in the fertile soil that was once being actively farmed.
name: Poorly-tended Fields

desc:
The gently-rolling grassland is broken by several wildly-overgrown farm fields.
The fields, some of which are surrounded by low rock walls, rise well above
their surroundings, owing to a combination of fast-growing weeds and fertile
soil. The overgrown crops still follow straight lines down each field, although
they are hard to make out in their current condition. To the east a clump of
trees rises from the grasslands, conspicuous for its height in an otherwise flat
area.
name: Poorly-tended Fields

desc:
The stifling aromas of rare and valuable herbs mingle together to form a smell
that overpowers the senses. An old map desk with many wide drawers in it has
been repurposed to become a counter in the centre of the room while sealed pots
and oil-filled jars are stacked around the edges of the shop.
name: Pungent Herbal Store

desc:
The ground here slopes down slightly in all directions, and rises again at a
distance; this rise and fall repeats as the land extends toward each horizon.
The effect is one of very gentle, rolling hills when viewed from far away and
only moderately uneven ground when viewed close-up. The occasional tree rises
from these gradual hillsides, conspicuously tall in an area that is otherwise
mostly flat.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
Long grasses cover these hills, appearing uniform at a distance but showing bare
patches close up. The soil in these bare patches is gravelly and weeds flourish
in some spots where the grass is sparse, the hardy weeds managing to find
nutrients in soil too barren for other plants to thrive. A few trees rise above
the gentle hills that stretch toward each horizon; of these, most rise from the
shallow valleys and folds that separate the hills.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
The grassy hills here are low and smooth, easily accessible from all directions.
Several small game trails zigzag their way through the tall grass, showing signs
of animal life. One hill sports an overhang, completely enshrouding the barren
earth beneath in shadow. Dead vines and ancient moss cling to an old, weathered
gravestone that had been placed with care long ago at the head of the mound.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
Small clusters of bushes float like little islands on the sea of grass flowing
over these hills while slim trees stand like majestic banners at the bottom of
shallow valleys. The rolling landscape gently levels off to flat grasslands in
the northwest. However, the grasslands lie considerably lower than these hills
and the descent is much too steep and long to climb. The rolling hills continue
uninterrupted to the east and south while in the north a farmhouse can be seen
amidst well-tended fields.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
Low hills roll gently out in all directions creating shallow valleys that become
hidden from view behind grass-flecked ridgelines. Sparse tree lines flank the
steeper slopes and follow several of the valleys; they blend together toward the
horizon, dark against the lighter-coloured hills they frame. Long grass grows
underfoot, thinning slightly atop many of the hills, varying in colour from tan
to yellow to green creating a pale, pastel blend of colours that stretches to
the horizon.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
The ground here is uneven, rising and falling to form low hills and shallow
valleys. Long grass covers the hills and fills the valleys with a uniform
greenery except for areas in several of the lower valleys where the ground
becomes rocky. These rocks are small; some are nothing more than gravel, spread
out in patches that have forced the grasses to grow more sparsely than usual.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
The humps and knolls of these hills are low and gentle. The rolling landscape
continues to the east and south while to the west and north, the hills give way
to rugged, almost vertical drop-offs to fields below. A farmhouse can be seen
far to the west on the fields below, surrounded by a tall fence. At the bottom
of a small valley, a shallow pond nurtures the nearby trees and shrubbery.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
Overgrown with vegetation, the hills continue to the north, west and south as
far as the eye can see. A solitary tree occupies the hilltop, most likely home
to the birds of the area. Occasionally, the cry of a bird of prey can be heard
as it attempts to hunt its quarry. Pieces of broken shale can be found amongst
the rocky outcrop near the tree.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
While the hills may initially appear grassy, the hard sediment under the surface
is formed from pieces of broken shale that shift underfoot. Progress amongst the
hills is slow as extra care must be taken not to slip on the shifting surface.
While no direct route exists to reach it, a heavily travelled path can be seen
to the south and east.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
The hills here are low and gentle and travelling is as effortless as can be
expected without road or trail to follow. To the south, the gentle hills are
interupted by a line of high, rocky ridges and no opportunity for travel in that
direction can be seen. To the north, east and west the rolling landscape with
its green knolls lie open.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
To the south, a steep ridge of jagged rock rises sharply cutting off passage in
that direction. Rolling hills flow to the east, west, and north like ripples on
a verdant, green pond. In a small valley between two hills, the remains of what
appears to be an old campsite can be seen.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
Here, tall, thick grass blankets the slopes, hilltops and shallow valleys alike.
Signs of grazing animals appear here and there. The gentle hills roll on to the
north and east while passage to the south is blocked by a steep ridge. To the
west, near the foothills, the land evens out to wide, flat plains.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
Tall, green grass covers this vast, rolling expanse. To the east and south, the
hills rise just high enough to block the view of what lies beyond. Other
vegetation is scarce, save for a few scattered but thriving groups of thorny
shrub. Westward and north, the gentle rolling hills suddenly give way to steep,
rocky slopes. At the bottom of these slopes, great flat fields stretch outwards
for miles. The fields to the west appear wild and untended while to the north
there appears to be some cultivation.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
The height differences between the valley floors and hill tops are rarely more
than a few dozen feet in these hills. The slopes up and down are gentle and
covered with tall grass. Vibrant patches of flowers in many colours seem to
explode across the hillsides, bringing contrast to an otherwise dull landscape.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
From the top of one of the taller hills in this region, the view is quite
spectacular. The Tower Hills cover a vast area and the rolling, grass-covered
landscape continues uninterrupted for miles in all directions. The ground is
dotted with small holes with rodents warily peeking out once in a while to see
if it is safe to come out and search for food.
name: Gentle Rolling Hills

desc:
A narrow cavern stretches far into the cliff. Underfoot the ground is stony but
a layer of sand covers most of the floor and deep, curving grooves adorn the
surface. At the base of the cavern wall lie the skeletal remains of a few small,
unfortunate mammals.
name: Dry Sandy Cavern

desc:
The walls of this cave have formed naturally in a jagged, sharp and uneven
fashion. However, the floor is surprisingly level. Fresh air seems to enter the
cave through a thin crack in the southern wall while the cave itself continues
on to the east.
name: Hollow in the Ridge

desc:
The uneven cavern walls are damp with moisture. Every now and then a drop falls
to the floor from the cavern's roof as water trickles in from above, albeit very
slowly. The floor of the cavern is considerably more even than its walls as if
some past effort has been made to level it. The cave ascends slightly as it
continues west while a steep slope leads deeper underground to the north.
name: Hollow in the Ridge

desc:
Walls of dark, brown stone press in from all sides, creating a narrow
tunnel-like cavern. Flat slices of shale lie shattered on the floor where it's
fallen from the roof and walls and more looks set to fall at any moment. Water
drips from the ceiling, wetting the dust-covered ground. Tiny, brittle bones lie
on the cavern's floor, covered by the dust. Most probably from bats or birds,
and likely very old.
name: Murky Cave

desc:
A long, narrow cave with walls of grey and brown shale has naturally formed
here. Faint sparkles of light hint at quartz and other minerals embedded in the
rock. The floor of the cave is smooth and even, making travel through the tunnel
easy to the north or west.
name: Murky Cave

desc:
Several narrow cracks and fissures high up on the northern cave wall hint at the
outside world. However, they are too small as well as too deep to contemplate
passage except by smaller creatures like bats or lizards. On the cavern's floor
lie many irregular piles of guano attesting bat infestation. Some of the piles
look disturbed or uneven, perhaps just by the rocks beneath, although in places
it looks like there's a sheen of metal beneath the guano. The rest of the floor
is surprisingly even and the walls seem to have crude shelves carved into them.
This cave may have served as a hideout or camp in the past.
name: Bat Nest

desc:
The valley that cut its way from the east ends here with only a hole in the
hillside to the south to be seen. The hole is large enough to allow entrance but
it isn't clear what would await inside. The hillsides to the west and north are
not ascendable. The valley floor is covered with grass and bushes.
name: End of a Valley

desc:
A tunnel begins here. It leads out to an area lush with vegetation to the north,
and deeper underground to the south. The tunnel looks to have been carved
through the bedrock by a river in the past. Wind-blown leaves litter the floor
of the tunnel and fungi clings to the grey stone walls.
name: Tunnel Entrance

desc:
A tunnel, low and narrow, leads through a bedrock of grey rock. The tunnel
slopes gently downwards to the south. While it's not clear from the featureless
tunnel what awaits at either end, the slope of the tunnel seems to indicate that
going south would lead deeper underground and north would lead towards the
outside world.
name: Sloping Tunnel

desc:
An underground crossroads has formed, with tunnels leading in all directions. To
the north, the tunnel slopes upward while in all other directions, they slope
downward. The air to the west seems to be more humid than in the other
directions, while the walls down the eastern tunnel are covered in fungi.
name: Intersection of Tunnels

desc:
The floor of this cave is littered with several small bones, as if something
frequents this cave often. A small stream runs down from the ceiling into a
small pool at the southern edge of this cavern, and the pool slowly seaps
through a crack under the southern wall. The opening into this wide underground
space lies to the north, where it appears that several tunnels intersect.
name: Wide Cave

desc:
The tunnel bends sharply to the east from the south here. The path to the south
slopes down sharply, and faint sounds of trickling water can be heard in that
direction. To the east, an upward sloping path can be followed. The air here
smells dank and damp, and the beginnings of moss and lichen can be seen clinging
to the rocky walls.
name: Bend in a Tunnel

desc:
This lake exists deep under the hills. The temperature of the water is low
enough that it feels chilly, but located as it is deep beneath the surface, it
is unlikely to freeze at winter. The lake is not large, a spear could be thrown
across it. Water trickles down from the ceiling at a slow but steady pace. There
are several large stalactites in a cluster near the centre of the lake.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
This tunnel is a long series of bends and turns, winding its way through the
bedrock. There are several small boulders scattered about on the ground, as if a
small collapse happened here long ago. Stalactites hang from the ceiling, but
the water that once formed them is no longer present. The tunnel slopes up to
the west, and winds out of sight to the south. The grey stone of the walls is
barely visible beneath layers of green and yellow fungi.
name: Long Winding Tunnel

desc:
The walls, floor and ceiling in this small cavern are covered in mould and
fungi, and a damp ripe stench fills the air. There are several moss covered
boulders here, and the droppings on the floor indicate that this is the home for
a colony of bats. A narrow tunnel to the north leads out of this cavern.
name: Fungus-infested Cavern

desc:
This air in this cavern is damp, and lichens grow on the rocks strewn on the
ground throughout it. The cave widens to the north where the dark, rippling
surface of an underground lake can be seen. There appears to be no way out from
here but daring the black waters.
name: Small Cavern

desc:
Narrow wooden stalls filled with fresh-cut hay provide basic but comfortable
accommodation for horses and mules of all kinds. Primitive but functional tack
for use on the horses has been stored on a wooden trestle against the outer
wall. A ladder leading to the loft space allows the stable-hands access to the
bales of straw stored there.
name: Black Hill Stables

desc:
A cobble stone path connects the back of the stables into an open field lined
with hedges on each side. The open field has been evenly divided into eight
garden plots, each lined with stones, and richly planted with a variety of
vegetables. A large pile of manure lies behind the stable and fills the air with
an earthy smell.
name: Garden behind a Stable

desc:
This simple but tidy home has only the most basic of furnitures. A large fire
place serves as a place to cook food and as a way to keep the house warm. By one
wall stands a sturdy wooden cupboard, and a small table with a pair of simple
chairs. There is only one window, and to either side of it there's a narrow bed,
suggesting that two people live in this house.
name: Simple Home

desc:
Three large farm-fields lie side by side just south of a small farmhouse. The
fields look like they are tended to regularly, and the plants grow in ordered
lines with no weeds in-between. A small pond borders the fields to the east
while more crops grow to the south on other fields.
name: Well-tended Fields

desc:
The ground slopes down from the north before levelling off into a flat and
well-tended farm field. Rows of crops have been sown in neat, north-south rows;
only a few small weeds have escaped the farmer's eye, growing low and close to
the ground. A simple stone wall forms a border to the field with a gap along the
southern edge that might have once housed a gate; here and there, stones have
fallen from the wall to sit in scattered piles at its base.
name: A Fishing Pond

desc:
This farm house is simple in design, consisting of only one large room. The
walls, ceiling and floor are constructed from tight-fitting but unfinished
wooden planks, their surfaces rough and somewhat uneven. The roof is peaked,
rising up in the centre, with wide beams crossing the empty space. Thick cobwebs
are visible among these rafters, offering a stark contrast to the rest of the
room, which is tidy and well-kept. A small table sits in the centre of the room
surrounded by three wooden chairs that are clearly hand-made.
name: Wooden Farm House

desc:
A large, grass-covered meadow extends over the slightly sloping field. The grass
is emerald green, about two feet tall, and occasionally sway slightly with the
wind. In the middle of the meadow, a copse of trees provides shade from the sun.
A simple wooden fence encircles the area.
name: Peaceful Meadow

desc:
A wide wheat fields slopes lazily up and down as it extends over the area. The
wheat itself is yellow-brown, almost golden in its colour, and grows about two
feet tall. Next to the field, a small path runs to the north, towards a cottage
that stands there amongst a group of trees.
name: Wheat Fields

desc:
This small wooden farmhouse consists of but a single room crammed with
furniture. A large fireplace dominates the northern wall, its hearth occupying a
significant portion of the dwelling's floor space; a bed, table, chairs and
several small cabinets occupy the remainder. The effect is claustrophobic; the
chairs must be pushed under the table to move past, and care must be taken to
avoid knocking into furniture when moving from one part of the room to another.
name: One-room Farmhouse

desc:
This round windmill has one light and spacious room. In the centre of the room
stands a big wooden trough for the milled flour, with a wooden pipe coming from
upstairs above it. Next to the door stands a group of furniture; a table, some
chairs and a cabinet. A narrow staircase leads to the upper floor, while the
mill's outer door is on the southern wall.
name: Inside an Old Windmill

desc:
The upper floor of the windmill is dominated by two round millstones that lie on
top of each other. The millstones are truly huge, approximately two yards wide,
and around one feet thick apiece. Around the millstones, various axles and
cogwheels fill the most of the space of this room. Narrow stairs, and a wooden
pipe for flour, lead downstairs, while the thickest axle leads through the roof
to a room above.
name: By the Millstones

desc:
This small round room exists at the top floor of the mill. A narrow level
platform makes a full circle around the room, encircling the mill's engine. The
engine is a complex-looking aggregation of cogwheels and axels, that get their
power from the wings of the mill, and move it forward to make the millstones
move. There is a small hole in the wall, from where one can see the four wooden
wings of the mill.
name: Top Floor of a Windmill

desc:
The huge doorway to the east has a high threshold that keeps seed and grains
from blowing out as well as dirt or leaves from blowing in. A smooth, wooden
floor spans the entire width of the barn. The ceiling is high and triangular in
shape. Long beams of timber create a sturdy foundation and wooden planks
complete the construction. A more regularly-sized door to the north separates
the barn from the living area of this small farm.
name: Lofty Timbered Barn

desc:
In this cosy house a gambrel roof rests on thick, sturdy beams of wood,
obviously built to last. It has one big room, with an open fireplace in the
centre that emits enough heat to keep the whole house warm. Next to the mantle
is a wooden table and four stout chairs. Some cutlery, plates and a candelabra
rest on it. A small loft sits about six feet up, along the western wall. It has
four straw mattresses lying next to each other. Small glass windows offer views
of the lush fields outside. The southern door leads into another structure; a
barn or granary.
name: Inside a Tidy Farmhouse

desc:
Rickety stairs lead from a dirty cellar floor to a wooden hatch overhead. Small
cobwebs have formed elaborate tapestries on the walls and rat droppings are
scattered about alongside some straw. Small cracks adorn the walls, some
partially hidden by a few torn cotton sacks that have been discarded. A lantern
was installed at some point, but now it lies broken on the floor.
name: Cellar Entrance

desc:
Mildewy walls and a dirty floor form a poor base for a root cellar. Several bags
once-filled with grain feature bite-sized holes with several small pieces of
chaff littering the ground next to them. Numerous narrow cracks can be found in
the walls, some of them boarded up with various small wooden planks nailed
together. A few barrels have been piled up against the eastern wall. One barrel
lies on its side resting in a small pool of water, with a small crack visible.
name: Root Cellar

desc:
Crates of all sizes have been piled up neatly, although a few of them have
broken. Those who have, have been tossed haphazardly against the northern wall.
A few hooks have been installed, a place where tools of the trade are hung. The
floor may have been clean at one point in time, but now suffers from a rodent
infestation, as rat droppings are scattered in a route that leads from rathole
to rathole. The remains of a lantern has been left on a large crate.
name: Storage Cellar

desc:
A large hole overhead is one of the most defining features of this tunnel. A
couple of broken wooden planks have been tossed into a few empty crates. Several
other planks of wood have been nailed to the wall, close to the floor. All of
the wood features many splinters and bite-marks, with an unpleasant odour
accompanying a few brown and yellow piles of goo that stick to the wood.
name: Storage Cellar

desc:
Gnawed bones, chewed strips of leather and a greal of straw make a giant nest
that covers most of a small tunnel. A few decomposing rat corpses are resting on
top of the nest, their unpleasant odour mixing with the stale air. A large beam
half sticks out of the dirt, with some of the wood gnawed showing a pile of
splinters growing on one side of the nest. A myriad of different sized holes are
found in the tunnel walls, passageways for small rodents only.
name: Rat's Nest

desc:
A small tunnel is a veritable mishmash of straw, randoms strips of cloth, dirt
mounds and scraps of wood, forming a large rat's nest. By chance, the tunnel is
large enough for a humanoid to enter, although only few would do so with ease. A
mostly-rotten beam forms the centre of the tunnel, a remnant of an earlier
structure that was abandoned long ago. Several wooden boards, some cracked and
rotten, cover the southern wall.
name: Rat's Nest

desc:
Crates made of rotted wood and mouldy bags of cotton are tossed onto a pile.
Cobwebs both big and small form veritable banners that line the tunnel, the
insects that have fallen prey to them are nothing more but dried husks. One
larger web features the dried corpse of a small rat. The ground below is
littered with droppings and tiny gnawed bones in several small piles.
name: Disused Cellar

desc:
The tunnel is only five feet high, forcing bent heads and knees for many
adventurers. A few wooden support beams, covered in grey and white webs, hold
the tunnel in place. Sharp cuts mar the beams, and several lines and circles
have been drawn upon them. A few broken tools, their wood rotten and rusted
beyond repair, have been ditched against the southern wall.
name: Underground Tunnel

desc:
The bare bedrock lies no more than a few feet away to either side in this small,
narrow cleft. It's not even ten feet deep at the far end and at the other end,
it could easily be walked out of without even needing to climb. Dense shrubs
grow above the cleft, closing over it almost like a lid.
name: Narrow Cleft

desc:
This cottage has been made out of stone. The walls are thick, although the
construction work doesn't look especially smooth and some rough edges of the
stones used protrude inwards. There are some basic furniture, looking old and
long abandoned, and a fireplace in the room. The door is on the southern wall
while four small windows offer a magnificent view down to the surrounding
valley.
name: Stone Cottage

desc:
The walls and the low-hanging roof of this small cellar are made of stone,
supported by a number of wooden beams. The room is few yards wide in either
direction, leaving only a small amount of space to move around. There is a
wooden shelf with some boxes and other minor items on it. A cramped alcove lies
to the north and small ladders lead up to a trapdoor in the ceiling.
name: Dusty Cellar

desc:
A small narrow alcove stretches from the south, continues a few yards, and then
stops at a grey rock wall. Apparently there have been shelves on the walls, but
aside of a few surviving fixtures only the barely-recognisable remains lie
scattered on the floor, littering the alcove. The floor is made out of smooth
rocks.
name: Cramped Alcove in a Dusty Cellar

desc:
This round underground chamber is about five yards across. The walls, roof, and
ceiling are carved from the bedrock of the surrounding stone, but has been
skilfully reinforced where needed with square stone bricks. There are some
weapon racks and cabinets in the room indicating this place is an old armoury.
name: Hidden Armoury

desc:
This is the heart and centre of the village. Many residents crowd the square,
talking and laughing as they go about their business. A small market has been
set up here specialising in vegetables and other local produce. A rather large
building dominates the northern side of the square, a faded sign hanging from
its walls portrays a frothing beer mug that leaves no doubt as to its purpose.
Simple wooden stalls set on the eastern side provide shelter for mounts of all
kinds while to the south a busy shop offers all manner of items for travellers.
name: Black Hill Market Square

desc:
This square is a busy crossing point in the village. The eastern road leads off
towards the main square, while the northern route passes through a residential
area before reaching the agricultural fields. Here, the normal village noises
are replaced by the ringing of metal on metal coming from the blacksmith's shop
to the south.
name: Smithy Square

desc:
A lattice of giant wood beams provides support to this damp underground dig. The
rocky walls are cut to an expertly smooth sheen, and small rivulets of water
trace their way along tiny imperfections, to the smooth floor below. On the
western wall, the ground slopes sharply upward, where a pair of giant rocks
provides a barrier against wind, and the elements.
name: Damp Mine

desc:
A wooden lattice extends from the west, providing protection from cave-ins, and
a place to hang tools and dry clothes. The black, rocky walls here are cut to an
expertly fine sheen. To the east an uneven wall blocks further travel, and a
small pile of rubble lays at the base of the wall. The air is stagnant here,
with notes of dirt and mildew.
name: Damp Mine

desc:
Several small trees rise from the surrounding grasslands, conspicuous for their
height in an area that is otherwise flat. The trees are spaced some distance
from each other, their branches barely touching. Near the trunk of the largest
tree the grass has been worn down to dirt and a simple fire pit, ringed with
stones, has been built in the very centre of the clearing with a small tent
erected alongside.
name: Small Grove of Trees

desc:
Unmistakably this is the home of a once-great warrior. A warg-fur rug is
stretched out in front of the hearth and a selection of battered weapons hang on
the walls. A particularly large flail stands out from the rest and can only have
been made for a troll, presumably one killed by the home's owner. A plain desk
sits against the far wall while a rocking chair is given pride of place in front
of the fire.
name: Retired Warrior's Home

desc:
This is the backyard of the blacksmith's workshop and a wooden canopy extends
from the side of the building to provide shelter over part of the yard. Beneath
the canopy the raw materials required by the smith have been piled; coal for the
furnace, bars of iron and pre-cut wooden poles to be used as handles for tools.
name: Blacksmith's Backyard

desc:
While the shop may be small it is packed from floor to ceiling with all manner
of items, most of them stored in dusty boxes upon wooden shelves. It certainly
seems as if few people have bought much of the shopkeeper's stock recently.
Hooks screwed into the roof timbers have been used to hang leather goods which
gives the shop its characteristic smell.
name: Cluttered Traveller's Shop
